clean_story:
Life’s only certainty. The end of biology.

The word death comes from Old English dēaþ, meaning the permanent stop of all body functions—like a clock’s gears freezing mid-tick, never to turn again.

It begins with cells starving. No oxygen, no fuel. Within minutes, the brain’s neurons, once crackling with electricity, fall silent. Muscles stiffen as proteins lock in place, turning limbs rigid like overcooked pasta. Then decay takes over: enzymes digest organs from within, while bacteria and fungi feast, recycling flesh into soil or gas. A buried body skeletons in 10–15 years; in air, it may take half that time.

Yet death isn’t just rot. It’s a trigger. Some mushrooms sprout only from decomposing wood. Fireflies sync their flashes over carcasses. Even human cells cheat it—HeLa cells, taken from a woman’s tumor in 1951, still divide in labs today, outliving her by decades.

Three related subjects are near-death experiences, cellular immortality tricks, and how cultures bury the dead.
